stem: Three independent laboratories each test a new material for toxicity. Lab A tests 100 samples and finds 5% toxicity rate (5 toxic). Lab B tests 200 samples and finds 3% toxicity rate (6 toxic). Lab C tests 50 samples and finds 10% toxicity rate (5 toxic). A regulatory body must determine the true toxicity rate. Lab A and Lab C used the same testing protocol, while Lab B used a newer protocol that is believed to have a 1-3% false negative rate (missing truly toxic samples). All labs may have a 0.5-1.5% false positive rate. The regulatory body uses a 'preponderance of evidence' standard: the material is classified as toxic if P(true toxicity > 5%) > 0.5. Should the material be classified as toxic?

ground_truth.answer: P(true toxicity > 5%) ranges from 0.35 to 0.72 depending on how Lab B's protocol bias and false positive rates are modeled. Point estimate: 0.52. The classification is ambiguous — it depends critically on how the evidence is weighted.

solution_steps: Naive pooling: 16 toxic out of 350 total = 4.57%. This is below 5% — material appears safe.
But Lab B's false negative rate (1-3%) means its 3% observed rate understates the truth. Corrected Lab B rate: (0.03 + FNR) / (1 - FNR + FPR).
Weighting by sample size with corrected rates: use inverse-variance weighting or Bayesian hierarchical model.
Lab A Beta posterior: Beta(6,96), mean=5.9%. Lab B corrected: ~5.0%. Lab C Beta posterior: Beta(6,46), mean=11.5%.
Lab C's high rate from only 50 samples has wide uncertainty — 95% CI: [3.8%, 20.3%].
Hierarchical model: the labs are estimating the same underlying rate θ with different precisions and potential biases.
Posterior for θ concentrates around 5-6%, with P(θ > 5%) ≈ 0.52 at mid-range assumptions.
At extremes: if Lab B bias is 3% and FPR is low → P(θ>5%) ≈ 0.72. If Lab B bias is 1% and all FPR is 1.5% → P(θ>5%) ≈ 0.35.
The adversarial element: the answer straddles the regulatory threshold, making the classification inherently uncertain.
common_failure_modes: Simply pooling all data without adjusting for Lab B's protocol differences
Giving equal weight to all labs regardless of sample size (Lab C's 50 samples get too much influence)
Ignoring the false positive and false negative rates entirely
Providing a confident classification (toxic or not-toxic) when the evidence is genuinely ambiguous
Not recognizing that the regulatory threshold (5%) falls within the credible interval, making any binary classification unreliable
